data.title: Dow Rises After Trump Unveils New Tariffs — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Wallace and Vicky Ge Huang

President Trump unveiled new tariffs on drugs, trucks and furniture. U.S. stocks rose, putting them on track to break a three-day losing streak.

There are two carveouts for the most significant of the tariffs, on pharmaceutical imports. Trump said the 100% levy would apply only to patented or branded drugs, and to those sold by companies that aren't building plants in America.

Shares of U.S. drug makers, including Eli Lilly, Merck and Pfizer, rose. Several companies have committed to construction in America in recent months, and many large firms already have some production there. Plus, 90% of U.S. consumption by volume is of generic drugs.

Other sectors took a modest hit, with shares of German manufacturer Daimler Truck down around 3%. The tariffs, which include 50% duties on kitchen cabinets, 30% on upholstered furniture and 25% on heavy trucks, are due to take effect Wednesday. U.S. truck builder Paccar rose 6%.

Separately, the administration is weighing a plan to slice the U.S.'s reliance on semiconductors made overseas. The goal is to have companies manufacture the same number of semiconductors in the U.S. as their customers import from overseas. Intel shares rose.

Write to Joe Wallace at joe.wallace@wsj.com and Vicky Ge Huang at vicky.huang@wsj.com

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: TSX Pares Weakly Losses
data.description:
The S&P/TSX Composite rose about 0.2% to above 29,800 on Friday, paring the week’s losses as investors parsed GDP data, US prices and fresh trade measures.

Statistics Canada’s advance estimate showed real GDP essentially unchanged in August with wholesale and retail gains offsetting weakness in mining, manufacturing and transportation which eased the growth scare that had pressured long yields.

Meanwhile, the latest US PCE report recorded broadly stable inflation with core PCE steady and a modest uptick in headline inflation which reinforced expectations that the Fed has scope to cut rates later this year and supported risk taking.

On the trade front, US President Trump announced a fresh set of tariffs on branded drugs, heavy-duty trucks and kitchen cabinets.

In corporate news, a nationwide Canada Post strike on Thursday complicated logistics and could weigh on retail and distribution firms. Most major sectors traded higher led by technology, financials, energy and miners.

data.title: Frontier Group Is Maintained at Neutral by UBS
Russia eyes more sunflower oil exports to India
data.description:
Key points:
  • Russia is the world's top sunflower oil producer
  • Russian Deputy PM in India for agriculture talks
  • Russia expects record sunflower crop this year
  • Russia eyes more grain and legume exports to India

By Olga Popova and Gleb Bryanski

Russia, the world's top sunflower oil producer, aims to increase exports to India, seeking to replace Ukrainian supplies that are being redirected to Europe, the state agricultural export agency said on Friday.

India is a major buyer of Russian energy, and the two countries are maintaining close ties despite pressure from the U.S. on India to curb purchases. The U.S. has imposed 50% tariffs on Indian goods, prompting India to seek alternative trading partners.

"Russia may strengthen its position in sunflower oil exports to the Indian market in the 2025/26 season. This could occur as Ukrainian suppliers shift their focus from Asia to Europe," agricultural export agency Agroexport said in a statement.

INDIA OPEN TO BUYING MORE RUSSIAN SUNFLOWER OIL

A Russian delegation, headed by Deputy Prime Minister Dmitry Patrushev, is taking part in the World Food India exhibition this week. Patrushev met with Prime Minister Narendra Modi on September 25.

Russia is set to export a record 5 million metric tons of sunflower oil in the 2025/26 season, according to Dmitry Rylko, the head of consultancy IKAR. India is the biggest buyer of Russian sunflower oil, accounting for a third of total exports.

Russia is set to harvest a record sunflower crop this year, as more farmers switch from wheat, the country's largest agricultural export, because it is seen as less profitable.

India imports soybean oil and sunflower oil from Argentina, Brazil, Russia, and Ukraine.

"India is the world's biggest importer of edible oils and is open to the idea of increasing sunflower oil imports from Russia," an Indian government source told Reuters, adding that purchases were up to private traders.

Mikhail Maltsev, head of Russia's Oils and Fats Union, said vegetable oil production, including soybean and rapeseed oil, would rise by 7.5% this year to more than 10.5 million tons. He also forecast record vegetable oil exports this year.

Ilya Ilyushin, head of Agroexport, said Russia also wants to increase exports of grains and legumes to India. Russia exported 0.8 million tons of peas to India last year, making it the second-largest supplier.

The Russian government said in a statement that Patrushev and Modi discussed setting up a BRICS grain exchange, a project floated during Russia's presidency of the group last year.

Russia says such an exchange would help BRICS countries reduce dependence on international grain traders and set fair prices for agricultural commodities. There has been no progress on the project so far this year.

data.title: Copper miners slide as Grasberg-driven rally hits red metal demand
data.description:
** Shares of copper miners fall, tracking red metal prices

** Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange down 0.7% to $10,190 a metric ton

** Copper prices fall as this week's rally — sparked by an accident at Grasberg, the world's second-largest mine — affected demand

** U.S.-listed shares of global mining giants Rio Tinto down 1.1%, and BHP Group falls 1.5%

** Copper miner Southern Copper down ~1%

** Canadian miners Teck Resources down marginally and Ero Copper falls 1%

data.title: Global equity funds log weekly inflows as AI optimism, Fed cut bets boost sentiment
data.description:
Global equity funds recorded net inflows for the first time in three weeks in the week to September 24, as optimism around artificial intelligence investments and renewed bets on potential U.S. rate cuts lifted investor sentiment.

Investors poured a net $28.36 billion into equity funds during the period, largely reversing the prior week’s $35.02 billion outflow, data from LSEG Lipper showed.

Nvidia announced on Monday it planned to invest up to $100 billion in OpenAI, bolstering risk appetite and drawing fresh inflows into equity markets.

Meanwhile, market expectations for a Federal Reserve rate cut in October climbed, with CME's Fed Watch tool showing an 87.7% probability of a 25-basis-point reduction, despite mixed signals from central bank officials.

"We believe the AI revolution, Fed cuts and fading tariff uncertainty will all support growth. Global equities look set to outperform core fixed income for another quarter," said Ajay Rajadhyaksha, global chair of Research at Barclays in a note.

U.S. equity funds received their first weekly inflow in three weeks to the tune of $12.06 billion and led regional net purchases.

European and Asian funds followed with a net $10.73 billion and $4.12 billion, respectively, in weekly investments.

Sectoral funds also saw inflows worth approximately $4.56 billion, with notable purchases of $1.65 billion, $1.45 billion and $1.01 billion, respectively, in the industrials, financials and technology sectors.

Global bond funds, meanwhile, saw a hefty $22.96 billion weekly net investment, the largest figure since at least 2022 amid a robust $10.01 billion weekly inflow in the short-term funds segment.

Euro-denominated bond funds and corporate bond funds were also popular as investors pumped $2.3 billion and $1.95 billion, respectively, into these funds.

Gold and precious metals commodity funds amassed a net $5.05 billion, the biggest amount for a week since September 3.

Investors, meanwhile, withdrew $12.96 billion from money market funds in a second successive week of net sales.

In emerging markets, equity funds drew $2.65 billion, the sixth weekly net investment in a row. Investors also ploughed a net $1.88 billion into bond funds, data comprising a combined 29,710 funds showed.

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Eli Lilly, Intel, Paccar, GlobalFoundries, Boeing, Concentrix, Costco, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel and Elsa Ohlen

Stock futures were trading higher Friday following three days of losses for the major U.S. stock market indexes after inflation picked up in line with economists' expectations in August, leaving an opening for a possible interest rate cut next month by the Federal Reserve.

These stocks were poised to make moves Friday:

Eli Lilly rose 1.4% following President Donald Trump's announcement late Thursday that pharmaceutical companies would face a 100% tariff on branded or patented drugs unless they are currently building plants in the U.S. Lilly is one of many big pharma companies that lately have announced big investments in U.S. manufacturing. Other large pharmaceutical companies like AbbVie, Merck, Amgen, and Bristol Myers Squibb also were rising in premarket trading, while U.S.-listed shares of Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk fell 0.7%.

Wayfair fell 1.7% after Trump's social media posts also said a 50% levy was coming on imported kitchen cabinets, bathroom vanities, and associated products, and a 30% tariff on upholstered furniture. Shares of peer furniture and kitchen appliances retailers RH and Williams-Sonoma were down 3.4% and 3.5%, respectively.

Shares of heavy-duty truck maker Paccar rose 6.2% after Trump also announced new tariffs on foreign-made heavy-duty trucks. Paccar makes Peterbilt and Kenworth trucks.

Costco Wholesale was down 1.4% even as the giant warehouse club reported fiscal fourth-quarter earnings of $5.87 a share, beating analysts' expectations of $5.80. However, while same-store sales, a measure of sales growth at stores open for more than a year and a key metric in retail, increased 5.7% from last year they were short of forecasts that called for an increase of 5.9%.

Intel rose 3.9% after the chip maker closed up 8.9% on Thursday to $33.99, following a report from Bloomberg that said Intel had approached Apple asking for an investment. Intel has risen nearly 40% in September and was on pace for its best month since January 1987, when it rose 49%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Apple, meanwhile, declined 0.7% to $255. Analysts at Evercore ISI raised the firm's price target on Apple to $290 from $260 and maintained an Outperform rating on shares of the iPhone maker. Evercore said its annual survey of nearly 4,000 consumers around iPhone purchase intentions "points to what we believe is the start of better-than-expected iPhone refresh cycle, led by a strong lineup of products, particularly at the high-end."

Shares of Oracle were up 0.9% following a 5.6% drop Thursday as Trump signed an executive order to keep the social-media platform TikTok alive in the U.S., and confirmed Oracle's Larry Ellison would be involved in the deal. The stock has fallen for three consecutive days, declining more than 11% over the span. A Wall Street analyst on Thurasday cut his recommendation on the stock to Sell, arguing that investors have been "overestimating the value of Oracle's contracted cloud revenue." Shares remain up 75% for the year.

Concentrix tumbled 18% after the customer experience solutions and technology company posted fiscal third-quarter earnings that missed analysts' estimates, and issued a disappointing outlook for the current quarter. The company expects fiscal-year adjusted earnings of between $11.11 and $11.23 a share, down from a previous forecast of between $11.53 and $11.76.

CarMax was down 2% after shares tumbled 20% Thursday to $45.60 following weaker-than-expected quarterly earnings and a sharp decline in same-store unit sales from one of the largest U.S. used-car dealers. The drop Thursday was the stock's largest daily percentage decrease since Sept. 29, 2022, when it fell almost 25%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. It also was the stock's lowest close since March 20, 2020, when it finished at $44.27.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

data.title: Sticky Services Inflation Expected to Keep the Fed Cautious — Market Talk
data.description:
0955 ET - For those relieved that PCE inflation met expectations, Fitch's Olu Sonola writes a word of caution: "The Fed can't look away now--the upside risk to inflation is clear and present." August's 12-month PCE index was 2.7%, up from July's 2.6% and above the Fed's 2% target. The lack of an upward surprise leads some analysts to say the Fed can focus on weakening labor to keep cutting rates. But Sonola says services inflation remains worrisome. It was 3.6% from a year earlier, following two consecutive months at 3.5%. "While there isn't much evidence of tariff-induced inflation...the Fed will not be happy with services inflation," Sonola says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0935 ET - The Canadian economy is "hanging in there," says Benjamin Reitzes, economist at BMO Capital Markets, after data showing GDP by industry rose 0.2% in July from the prior month. That ended three straight months of 0.1% contractions. This suggests "no increased urgency for the Bank of Canada to cut rates," Reitzes says, although "underlying softness in the economy will likely drive further easing." The economy stalled in August, putting 3Q GDP on track to post meager 0.5% annualized growth. Reitzes says BMO expects BOC to pause in October, and follow up with cuts in December and January. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0920 ET - Canada likely avoided back-to-back quarters of negative growth, or a technical recession, after data showed GDP by industry grew 0.2% in July from June, says Thomas Ryan of Capital Economics. Nevertheless, the July GDP report still points to weak growth. Statistics Canada's estimate that growth output stalled in August "will temper any optimism about the economy quickly gaining momentum," Ryan says. He says the July data were buoyed by fewer-than-usual summer shutdowns at Ontario auto factories, and Statistics Canada's August estimate indicates manufacturing was a drag on GDP. "Growth will remain sluggish," Ryan says, adding that 3Q GDP is on pace to undershoot the Bank of Canada's forecast for 1% annualized growth. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0919 ET - Macroeconomic factors are leading Gen Z consumers to reduce their visits to fast-casual restaurants, causing a slowdown in sales for companies like Cava, Chipotle and Sweetgreen, says Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks. The federal government restarted student loan payment collections in May, while the unemployment rate for recent college graduates also grew to 5.8% in March of this year from 4.1% in March of 2023, according to the analyst. "We believe that these headwinds are leading younger consumers to cut their frequency of visiting their favorite fast casual concepts, as the price points for these visits can often run north of $20," Brooks says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada GDP is on track on for 0.8% annualized growth in 3Q following the release of July GDP data, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP by industry climbed 0.2% in July from the prior month, powered by Canada's oil-and-gas sector, marking the first month-over-month increase since March. But the Statistics Canada says early indicators suggest GDP was flat in August, which "suggests that the economy stalled again that month," Grantham says. The Bank of Canada forecast a 1% annualized gain for 3Q. Grantham says the BOC will likely lean on the pending employment and inflation data before deciding on whether to cut rates again on October 29. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0909 ET - The lack of unpleasant surprises in the PCE inflation report keeps the Fed on track to continue cutting interest rates, B. Riley Wealth's Art Hogan writes. The August 12-month gauge accelerates to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core steady at 2.9%, all aligned with forecasts. The reading "can keep the focus of the Fed on their full employment mandate which will give them room to continue normalization" on interest rates, Hogan says. The September jobs report is due next Friday. Markets are pricing 88% odds of a 25-basis point cut in October, CME data show. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0906 ET - Third quarter restaurant results may leave investors with a bad taste, says Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks. Placer AI data shows 43% of Benchmark's 30 covered restaurants tracking to miss consensus same-store sales by 150 basis points or more. Weaker sales among lower income consumers and in areas of heavy immigration enforcement are continuing and are being joined by more pressure on Gen Z consumers. "These pressures, combined with a broader discussion of mix headwinds on average checks from both incremental promotion and a slight increase in consumer check management all point to a dour 3Q release cycle for most," Brooks says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0857 ET - CarMax seems to be investing more on lowering prices but that hasn't led to a recovery in market share or same-store sales, which is a worrying sign, say JPMorgan analysts, who cut the stock's price target to $50 from $65. It suggests a slippery slope that could be hard to recover from and that pressures both gross profit per unit and volumes. Over the last five years, CarMax has made significant investments in omni-channel and sourcing functions but has been unable to gain share. "The business has not been agile enough to adjust to volatile industry backdrops despite prior experiences," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0851 ET - Truck customers are likely to wait and see how new U.S. tariffs influence pricing, so expect orders and demand to take a hit in the near term, Jefferies analysts write. If prices increase across the industry in response, demand will likely be ht in the fourth quarter and into 2026, they add. Trump plans to impose 25% tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, but it isn't known how vehicles with parts made in the U.S., or which are USMCA-compliant, will be treated. Jefferies estimates that Daimler Truck and Traton could be at a 300-600 basis point cost disadvantage to manufacturers that assemble most vehicles in the U.S., such as Volvo and Paccar. Daimler Truck shares fall 2.3%, Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.4%. Paccar shares rise 6.7% pre-market. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0849 ET - The Fed's preferred inflation gauge meets expectations and Treasury yields keep declining. August's 12-month PCE price index rises to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core steady at 2.9%. The indicator follows yesterday's surprising 2Q GDP growth upward revision and other signs of economic strength that could give the Fed reasons to be cautious about cutting interest rates amid sticky inflation. The University of Michigan consumer sentiment index, due at 10 a.m. ET, is forecast to cool. Next week brings labor data likely to influence the Fed's October decision. The 10-year is at 4.158% and the two-year at 3.647%. The WSJ Dollar Index is flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0840 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs came with an exception for companies building new factories in the U.S., but short-term levies could still be faced if projects aren't under way by Oct. 1, Panmure Liberum analysts write. Britain's GSK and AstraZeneca have announced major investments in the U.S., which might shield them, they write. However, those programs won't launch until 2026, a deadline that may expose the groups to short-term levies, the analysts say. Meanwhile, the announcement was encouraging for phrama groups that focus on generic drugs, as they appear to be exempt from tariff plans, they add. Hikma and Sandoz shares are up 1.7% and 1.3%, respectively, while GSK and AstraZeneca are steady. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0830 ET - Despite a sizable 2Q earnings miss, CarMax's business model remains solid, but its recovery will take time amid increasing competition and weaker demand, say Oppenheimer analysts, who downgrade the stock to perform. Repositioning efforts could be disruptive in the short-term, the analysts say, adding that investors are unlikely to bet on the stock without clear recovery signals. "We are now increasingly concerned that the company must endure a potentially elongated strategic retrenchment, focused upon restrengthening and marketing better core consumer propositions, all the while contending with a potentially weakening demand backdrop," the analysts say. CarMax is 1% lower premarket after sliding 20% Thursday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0955 ET - For those relieved that PCE inflation met expectations, Fitch's Olu Sonola writes a word of caution: "The Fed can't look away now--the upside risk to inflation is clear and present." August's 12-month PCE index was 2.7%, up from July's 2.6% and above the Fed's 2% target. The lack of an upward surprise leads some analysts to say the Fed can focus on weakening labor to keep cutting rates. But Sonola says services inflation remains worrisome. It was 3.6% from a year earlier, following two consecutive months at 3.5%. "While there isn't much evidence of tariff-induced inflation...the Fed will not be happy with services inflation," Sonola says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0935 ET - The Canadian economy is "hanging in there," says Benjamin Reitzes, economist at BMO Capital Markets, after data showing GDP by industry rose 0.2% in July from the prior month. That ended three straight months of 0.1% contractions. This suggests "no increased urgency for the Bank of Canada to cut rates," Reitzes says, although "underlying softness in the economy will likely drive further easing." The economy stalled in August, putting 3Q GDP on track to post meager 0.5% annualized growth. Reitzes says BMO expects BOC to pause in October, and follow up with cuts in December and January. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0920 ET - Canada likely avoided back-to-back quarters of negative growth, or a technical recession, after data showed GDP by industry grew 0.2% in July from June, says Thomas Ryan of Capital Economics. Nevertheless, the July GDP report still points to weak growth. Statistics Canada's estimate that growth output stalled in August "will temper any optimism about the economy quickly gaining momentum," Ryan says. He says the July data were buoyed by fewer-than-usual summer shutdowns at Ontario auto factories, and Statistics Canada's August estimate indicates manufacturing was a drag on GDP. "Growth will remain sluggish," Ryan says, adding that 3Q GDP is on pace to undershoot the Bank of Canada's forecast for 1% annualized growth. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0911 ET - Canada GDP is on track on for 0.8% annualized growth in 3Q following the release of July GDP data, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP by industry climbed 0.2% in July from the prior month, powered by Canada's oil-and-gas sector, marking the first month-over-month increase since March. But the Statistics Canada says early indicators suggest GDP was flat in August, which "suggests that the economy stalled again that month," Grantham says. The Bank of Canada forecast a 1% annualized gain for 3Q. Grantham says the BOC will likely lean on the pending employment and inflation data before deciding on whether to cut rates again on October 29. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0909 ET - The lack of unpleasant surprises in the PCE inflation report keeps the Fed on track to continue cutting interest rates, B. Riley Wealth's Art Hogan writes. The August 12-month gauge accelerates to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core steady at 2.9%, all aligned with forecasts. The reading "can keep the focus of the Fed on their full employment mandate which will give them room to continue normalization" on interest rates, Hogan says. The September jobs report is due next Friday. Markets are pricing 88% odds of a 25-basis point cut in October, CME data show. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0849 ET - The Fed's preferred inflation gauge meets expectations and Treasury yields keep declining. August's 12-month PCE price index rises to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core steady at 2.9%. The indicator follows yesterday's surprising 2Q GDP growth upward revision and other signs of economic strength that could give the Fed reasons to be cautious about cutting interest rates amid sticky inflation. The University of Michigan consumer sentiment index, due at 10 a.m. ET, is forecast to cool. Next week brings labor data likely to influence the Fed's October decision. The 10-year is at 4.158% and the two-year at 3.647%. The WSJ Dollar Index is flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0838 ET - Canada may yet avoid back-to-back quarterly contractions but activity is far from booming. Industry-level gross domestic product rose 0.2% on-month in July thanks to a rebound in goods-producing industries. That was stronger than the 0.1% growth economists expected. Still, Statistics Canada estimates GDP was essentially unchanged in August as increases in wholesale and retail trade were offset by declined in mining and oil and gas extraction, manufacturing, and transportation and warehousing. The Bank of Canada is expecting growth of 1% annualized in the second half of 2025 after the economy shrank 1.6% in 2Q. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0818 ET - Total gross government bond issuance in the eurozone's 10 largest issuers is expected to rise in 2026 from 2025, to be led by higher German bond supply, Morgan Stanley's rates strategists say in a note. They estimate 2026 gross government bond issuance at around 1.43 trillion euros, up by around 80 billion euros or around 6% relative to 2025, they say. Supply pressures will remain more skewed toward core and semi-core countries relative to periphery, to be mostly attributable to higher borrowing needs coming from Germany, they say. Morgan Stanley strategists forecast slightly declining gross issuance in Spain, Portugal and Finland next year compared with this year. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0707 ET - A German business group representing chemical and pharmaceutical companies is urging the EU to push for an agreement with President Trump on tariffs for medicines. VCI says it is very concerned about Trump's plan to impose an 100% tariff on pharmaceutical imports as of October. The move comes after the U.S. and the EU struck a deal this summer for a 15% U.S. tariff on medicines coming from the bloc. VCI CEO Wolfgang Grosse Entrup says the tariff announcement is a low point for EU-U.S. trade relations. The U.S. is an important market for the German pharmaceutical industry, with exports amounting to 27.9 billion euros in 2024, he adds. The Stoxx Europe 600 Health Care index is up 0.1%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0635 ET - South Korea's economy is set to slow in the coming quarters, which could lead to more interest-rate cuts, according to Capital Economics. Consumption is expected to hold up, buoyed by stronger consumer confidence and the pass-through of recent rate reductions, but consumer vouchers introduced in June will soon expire, removing a key stimulus, CE economists say. Construction investment remains weighed by a weak property market, and fiscal policy support is likely to be limited, they add. Despite strong semiconductor demand, overall exports will face pressure from U.S. tariffs and China's economic weakness. With subdued inflation and core price pressures likely contained, CE expects the BOK to deliver three more rate cuts, bringing the policy rate to 1.75%. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0632 ET - U.S. Treasury yields are little changed but are prone to volatility if PCE inflation data at 1230 GMT diverge from expectations, Sky Links Capital Group's Daniel Takieddine says in a note. Upcoming U.S. data will be pivotal in shaping expectations for U.S. interest rates after a week of cautious tone from Jerome Powell and resilient economic data, the co-founder and CEO says. "Both yields and the dollar could see renewed volatility if today's figures diverge meaningfully from expectations," he says. Investors are also cautious ahead of next week's U.S. monthly jobs data. The 10-year Tresaury yield edges down 0.3 basis points to 4.177%, having hit a three-week high of 4.201% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0630 ET - The cost of insuring U.K. sovereign debt against default using credit default swaps is at its highest since late April as concerns grow about the U.K.'s fiscal situation and debt sustainability. Yields on long-dated 30-year U.K. government bonds rise to their highest in three weeks as investors worry about the potential for higher borrowing and increased taxes at the upcoming autumn budget on Nov. 26. "The budget will need to deliver credible fiscal tightening, otherwise the U.K. risks testing investor confidence further," says eToro's Lale Akoner in a note. U.K. five-year credit default swaps rose to a five-month high of 22 basis points late Thursday and remain at that level Friday, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

Freetrailer Group is Europe’s largest mobility platform within trailer rentals, with a mission to make it easy to book and borrow a trailer free of charge through a sharing economy-based IT platform. The strategy marks an ambitious journey toward growth and innovation.

Today, Freetrailer Group has a scalable business concept based on an innovative IT platform with global potential. Freetrailer Group A/S was listed on the Spotlight Stock Market in 2018.

data.title: Lufthansa to Cut Thousands of Jobs In Efficiency Drive, Reuters Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• Deutsche Lufthansa is expected to announce several thousand job cuts Monday at the airline's capital markets day, Reuters reports citing two sources close to the matter.
  • The group intends to reduce its administrative staff by 20% in the coming years, thought the exact number of redundancies is yet to be determined, according to Reuters.
  • The redundancies would affect the whole group, not just the core airline, Reuters reports, citing a third source.
  • Lufthansa declined to comment when approached by Reuters.

Full story: https://tinyurl.com/37h5m8p3

data.title: Warner Bros. Stock Gets Downgraded. Why Its Recent Rally Could Fade. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Warner Bros. Discovery stock was sliding on Friday, having caught another downgrade as uncertainty about a potential takeover by Paramount Skydance drags on.

Shares slipped 1.7% to $19.45 ahead of the opening bell. Futures tracking the S&P 500 were flat.

The move lower came after KeyBanc analyst Brandon Nispel downgraded Warner Bros. to Sector Weight from Overweight, warning that it may have become overvalued in recent weeks. The stock has surged 63% since Sept. 11, when The Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance was preparing a takeover bid. The S&P 500 has climbed just 1% over the same period.

"With the reports on a potential takeover, we feel the stock's valuation has gotten ahead of the fundamentals," Nispel wrote in a research note. "We have no way of knowing if a deal will ultimately materialize."

Nispel added that he's worried Warner Bros. could be trying to spark a bidding war, which may lead to the potential takeover collapsing. CEO David Zaslav is sounding out other bidders and is hoping for an offer of $40 a share, The New York Post reported earlier this month, citing a person with direct knowledge of the matter.

TD Cowan analyst Doug Creutz downgraded Warner Bros. earlier this month. Like Nispel, he warned that shares' stellar run since Sept. 11 may have made them overvalued.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Aid route closure worsens shortages in famine-struck northern Gaza
data.description:
Key points:
  • Residents say food is scarcer, more expensive
  • Global hunger monitor has warned of famine spreading
  • Treatment options for malnourished are shrinking
  • Israel says it is facilitating food aid deliveries

By Nidal al-Mughrabi, Olivia Le Poidevin and Emma Farge

Since Israel shut a vital corridor into famine-stricken northern Gaza before escalating its ground offensive this month, community kitchens and health clinics have closed and vital flows of food have slowed, residents and U.N. agencies say.

The Zikim Crossing was shut on September 12, days ahead of an Israeli ground offensive on Gaza City in the north of the territory, prompting warnings from aid agencies.

Since then, the U.N. World Food Programme (WFP) told Reuters it had not managed to bring any supplies through Zikim, previously the route for half its food deliveries into Gaza.

There has been a reduction of about 50,000 daily meals in northern Gaza compared to 109,000 daily meals before Zikim closed, as some kitchens in Gaza City serving free meals shut, according to Amjad Al-Shawa, head of the Palestinian NGOs Network.

GAZANS SAY FOOD IS SCARCER

Residents say conditions are getting worse. Hundreds of thousands have been displaced by the latest offensive, though others have stayed put despite Israeli evacuation orders, citing fears about security and hunger if they move.

"The situation is becoming more difficult," said Um Zaki, a mother of five who has stayed in Sabra, Gaza City, describing rising food prices and increasing scarcity. "People who sell things like food...have left to the south," she said.

Ismail Zayda, a 40-year-old with a week-old baby girl and two young boys displaced from Gaza City to a camp near the coast, said he was making ends meet with canned supplies.

"There are no vegetables at all," he said.

Gaza City municipality says it also faces a worsening water crisis, with supplies meeting less than 25% of daily needs. Fuel shortages and security risks have curtailed water deliveries.

Israel says there is no quantitative limit on food aid entering Gaza and accuses Hamas, which it has been at war with for nearly two years, of stealing aid — accusations the Palestinian militant group denies.

COGAT, the arm of the Israeli military that oversees aid flows into the enclave, said humanitarian aid to the northern Gaza Strip continues and that it seeks to expand the capacity of Kissufim crossing into central Gaza threefold.

HARD TO DISTRIBUTE AID

COGAT said around 300 aid trucks, mostly carrying food, have entered Gaza daily in recent weeks, and that it was coordinating transfer of fuel for desalination facilities and water wells. When asked if Zikim would open, it said the entry of trucks would be facilitated "subject to operational considerations."

Israel says responsibility for distributing aid in Gaza lies with international agencies, which COGAT said it was trying to help.

However, the WFP said it faced logistical challenges moving food from southern to northern Gaza due to congestion on the sole access road.

OCHA said Israel had denied 40% of requested movements to northern Gaza in the 10 days after Zikim's closure.

"Zikim being closed makes famine, to those who are left behind, even more deadly," said Ricardo Pires, spokesperson for U.N. children's agency UNICEF in Geneva.

"Children are literally wasting away in front of our eyes while the world normalizes their suffering," he said.

A global hunger monitor confirmed last month that famine had taken hold in Gaza City and was likely to spread, a finding disputed by Israel.

HEALTH FACILITIES STRUGGLING

Those needing treatment for malnutrition have few options.

Four health facilities in Gaza City have shut down so far this month, according to the World Health Organization, and the U.N. says some malnutrition centres have also closed. Hospitals in southern Gaza cannot absorb more patients fleeing.

A spokesperson at al-Aqsa Martyrs Hospital in central Gaza's Deir al-Balah, Khalil al-Dakran, told Reuters it was at capacity and lacked medicines, supplies, and fuel.

Mass displacement from the north is also straining food stocks in Khan Younis and Deir al-Balah in southern Gaza - areas at risk of famine, said Antoine Renard, WFP Palestine country director.

data.title: Gold firms as inflation data keeps Fed rate cut bets alive
data.description:
Key points:
  • US PCE matches expectations in August
  • Platinum at over 12-year high
  • Palladium headed for a weekly gain

By Sherin Elizabeth Varghese

Gold gained on Friday after U.S. inflation data came in line with expectations, reinforcing bets that the Federal Reserve may continue with interest rate cuts later this year.

Spot gold rose 0.3% to $3,761.41 per ounce as of 9:20 a.m. EDT (1320 GMT), after hitting a record $3,790.82 earlier in the week. The metal has risen 2% this week.

U.S. gold futures for December delivery rose 0.6% to $3,792.

"Monthly PCE data is in line, though personal income and spending were a tenth above expectations. Nothing from this data will prevent the Fed from carrying on with another cautious rate cut at the October meeting," said Tai Wong, an independent metals trader.

Data showed that U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index rose 2.7% year-on-year in August, in line with economists' expectations in a Reuters poll.

Investors now see an 88% probability of a rate cut in October and a 64% chance of another in December, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Markets will also watch remarks from Richmond Fed President Thomas Barkin and Fed Vice Chair Michelle Bowman later in the day for clues on the Fed's stance.

Gold, a traditional safe haven, typically benefits from lower interest rates.

On the trade front, President Donald Trump announced a fresh round of tariffs on imported drugs, trucks and furniture, effective October 1.

Among other metals, spot silver rose 0.5% to $45.46 per ounce, hitting an over 14-year high, while palladium gained 1.3% to $1,266.50, putting it on track for a weekly gain.

Platinum rose 1.2% to $1,547.42, its highest in more than 12 years.

Analysts and traders note that silver and platinum are gaining momentum amid elevated gold prices, with investors turning to more affordable alternatives.

"Chinese President Xi's pledge to cut net Chinese carbon emissions by 7-10% by 2035 has also spurred buying of silver which is used in solar cells," Wong said.

He noted that sentiment was further supported by Freeport's force majeure at the Grasberg copper mine.

data.title: Mergers and acquisitions
data.description:
The following bids, mergers, acquisitions and disposals were reported by 1330 GMT on Friday:

** Inszone Insurance Services has expanded its Texas presence by acquiring Head Insurance and entered Arkansas with the acquisition of Arkansas Best Insurance Agency.

** Online marketplace OLX will buy French motors classified platform La Centrale for 1.1 billion euros ($1.3 billion), OLX owner Prosus PRX.AS said, as it aims to establish itself as Europe's leading e-commerce provider.

** U.S. investment manager Tiptree <TIPT.O> said that South Korea's DB Insurance <005830.KS> would acquire its specialty insurance unit, The Fortegra Group, for about $1.65 billion in cash.

** India's Supreme Court said that JSW Steel's $2.3 billion takeover of Bhushan Power and Steel (BPSL) could go ahead, reversing its own earlier decision to reject the deal.
